chunks.title: Software development life cycle
chunks.text: The SDLC covers the phases of building software: requirements gathering, design, implementation, testing, deployment, maintenance. The Waterfall model runs them sequentially and works when requirements are stable. Agile breaks work into short iterations (sprints) where each phase repeats, suiting volatile requirements. Spiral combines iterative development with formal risk analysis. DevOps extends agile to operations — continuous integration, deployment, and monitoring become part of the loop.

chunks.title: Agile and Scrum
chunks.text: Agile is a mindset (the Agile Manifesto: working software over documentation, responding to change over following a plan). Scrum is a popular framework. Work is broken into product backlog items; the team commits to a subset for a 1-4 week sprint. Daily standups surface blockers. A sprint review demos the increment; the retrospective improves the process. Roles: Product Owner (prioritizes backlog), Scrum Master (facilitates), Development Team. Kanban is a lighter alternative focused on flow.

chunks.title: Version control with Git
chunks.text: Git is a distributed version control system. Every clone has the full history. The basic workflow: edit files, git add (stage), git commit (record snapshot with a message), git push (send to remote). Branches let you work on isolated changes. git merge combines branches; git rebase replays commits onto a new base for a linear history. git pull fetches and merges. Pull requests (or merge requests) are the GitHub/GitLab convention for code review before merging.

chunks.title: Testing
chunks.text: Unit tests verify one function or class in isolation, fast and many. Integration tests verify multiple components working together. End-to-end tests drive the full system through its UI or API, slow but most realistic. The test pyramid says: lots of unit tests, fewer integration, very few E2E. TDD (test-driven development) writes the test first, watches it fail, writes minimal code to pass, refactors. Code coverage measures which lines tests exercise, not whether tests are good.

chunks.title: Continuous integration and deployment
chunks.text: CI (continuous integration) automatically builds and tests every commit, catching breakage early. CD (continuous delivery) ensures every passing build is releasable; continuous deployment goes further and ships every passing build to production automatically. Common CI services: GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Jenkins, CircleCI. A typical pipeline runs lint, type-check, unit tests, integration tests, builds artifacts, and deploys to staging or production.

chunks.title: Design patterns
chunks.text: Design patterns are reusable solutions to common design problems, named and catalogued in the Gang of Four book. Creational: Singleton, Factory, Builder. Structural: Adapter, Decorator, Facade, Composite. Behavioural: Strategy, Observer, Command, Iterator, State. Patterns are a shared vocabulary, not a checklist — use them when they clarify intent, not for their own sake. Overusing patterns leads to ceremony and indirection without value.

chunks.title: Requirements engineering
chunks.text: Requirements describe what the system must do (functional) and qualities like performance, security, usability (non-functional). Techniques: stakeholder interviews, user stories ('As a X I want Y so that Z'), use cases (numbered steps for a goal), prototypes. Requirements should be testable, unambiguous, and prioritized. The MoSCoW method splits them into Must / Should / Could / Won't. Bad or missing requirements are the most common cause of project failure.

chunks.title: SOLID principles
chunks.text: SOLID is a set of five OOP design principles. Single Responsibility: a class should have one reason to change. Open/Closed: open for extension, closed for modification. Liskov Substitution: subtypes must be substitutable for their base types without breaking the program. Interface Segregation: prefer many small interfaces over one large one. Dependency Inversion: depend on abstractions, not concretions. They guide maintainable OO design but aren't rigid laws.

chunks.title: DRY, KISS, YAGNI
chunks.text: DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) says every piece of knowledge should have one authoritative representation. KISS (Keep It Simple, Stupid) values simplicity over cleverness. YAGNI (You Aren't Gonna Need It) says don't build features for hypothetical future needs. These three principles cut against each other in practice: removing duplication can hurt simplicity; YAGNI fights premature abstraction. Use them as nudges, not laws.

chunks.title: Code smells
chunks.text: Code smells are surface signs of deeper design problems. Long methods, large classes (god objects), long parameter lists, duplicate code, feature envy (a method that uses another class more than its own), shotgun surgery (one change requires edits in many places), divergent change (one class changes for many reasons). Smells are heuristics — sometimes the simpler version is fine. Refactoring catalogues (Fowler) describe matching fixes for each smell.

chunks.title: Refactoring techniques
chunks.text: Refactoring is changing code structure without changing behaviour. Classic refactorings: Extract Method (pull a code block into its own function), Rename Variable, Inline Variable, Replace Magic Number with Constant, Introduce Parameter Object (group related parameters), Move Method (to the class that uses its data). Always run tests after each step. Modern IDEs automate most of these safely.

chunks.title: Code review best practices
chunks.text: Code reviews catch bugs, share knowledge, and maintain coding standards. Good practices: keep PRs small (< 400 lines), self-review before submitting, focus on logic and design over style (linters handle style), comment to suggest not demand, approve when you'd accept the consequences, ship within a day. Reviewers benefit from clear PR descriptions explaining the why. Pair programming substitutes synchronous review.

chunks.title: Pair programming
chunks.text: Pair programming has two developers at one keyboard: a driver who types and a navigator who reviews and thinks ahead. Pairs catch bugs in real time, transfer knowledge, and avoid drift. Costs are obvious (two people on one task) but the throughput often exceeds two solo developers because of fewer rework loops. Modern remote tools (VS Code Live Share, tmate) make remote pairing practical.

chunks.title: DevOps and DevSecOps
chunks.text: DevOps merges development and operations into one team responsible for an application's full lifecycle. Practices: infrastructure as code, automated testing, continuous deployment, monitoring as code, post-mortem culture. DevSecOps adds security throughout — shift-left scanning, threat modelling in design, dependency vulnerability checks in CI, runtime intrusion detection. The goal is to make security a continuous concern, not a gate at the end.

chunks.title: Feature flags
chunks.text: Feature flags (toggles) let you deploy code that's hidden behind a runtime check. You can roll out gradually to 1%, 10%, 100% of users; A/B test; kill switches for problems; let product launch dates differ from engineering deploys. Risks: flag debt (flags accumulate forever), flag interactions, and accidentally exposing unfinished features. Tools: LaunchDarkly, Unleash, hand-rolled config flags.

chunks.title: Trunk-based development vs GitFlow
chunks.text: Trunk-based development has everyone committing to one main branch, behind feature flags for incomplete work. Releases cut from trunk. It minimises long-lived branches and merge pain. GitFlow uses develop, feature/*, release/*, hotfix/*, and master branches with strict merge rules. GitFlow worked for shrink-wrapped software releases; trunk-based fits continuous deployment. Most modern teams use trunk-based with short-lived feature branches and PRs.

chunks.title: Semantic versioning
chunks.text: SemVer (semantic versioning) uses MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH. Bump MAJOR for breaking changes, MINOR for backwards-compatible features, PATCH for backwards-compatible bug fixes. Pre-release tags (1.0.0-beta.1) mark unstable versions. Adopting SemVer makes dependency upgrades predictable — npm and Cargo can auto-update patch and minor versions but never major. Calendar versioning (2026.5) is an alternative for products without library users.

chunks.title: Documentation as code
chunks.text: Documentation should live in version control alongside the code it describes, written in plain text (Markdown, AsciiDoc) and built by CI like any artifact. Benefits: docs review like code, branches keep docs synchronized with features, broken links and outdated info show up as CI failures. Tools: MkDocs, Sphinx, Docusaurus, mdBook. Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) document why important choices were made.

chunks.title: API documentation and OpenAPI
chunks.text: OpenAPI (formerly Swagger) is a specification format for REST APIs: paths, parameters, request/response schemas, auth schemes. From an OpenAPI spec you can auto-generate documentation, client SDKs in many languages, server stubs, and mock servers for testing. Tools: Swagger UI for interactive docs, Redoc for static, openapi-generator for codegen. GraphQL APIs use SDL for the same purpose.

chunks.title: Backward compatibility
chunks.text: Backward-compatible changes don't break existing clients: adding optional fields, adding endpoints, expanding enums, making required fields optional. Breaking changes do: removing fields, renaming, changing types, changing default behaviour. Strategies for breaking changes: versioned APIs (/v1, /v2), deprecation periods with warnings, content negotiation. Internally, expand-then-contract migrations let you change schemas without downtime.

chunks.title: Microservices vs monoliths
chunks.text: A monolith deploys as one unit — simpler to develop, debug, and deploy in early stages. Microservices split functionality into independent services — each can scale, deploy, and use different tech independently. Costs: distributed-system pain (network failures, latency, eventual consistency), operational complexity (many things to monitor), data integrity across services is hard. Start monolithic; extract services only when team or scale demands it.

chunks.title: Domain-driven design
chunks.text: DDD models software around the business domain. Bounded contexts isolate models that mean different things in different parts of the business (a 'Customer' in billing vs support). Ubiquitous language ensures domain experts and developers use the same terms. Entities have identity; value objects don't. Aggregates enforce consistency boundaries. DDD shines on complex domains; it's overkill for CRUD apps.

chunks.title: Hexagonal and clean architecture
chunks.text: Hexagonal (ports-and-adapters) architecture isolates the domain core from external concerns (UI, database, messaging) via interfaces. Clean Architecture organises layers from innermost (entities) outward (use cases, interface adapters, frameworks) with dependencies pointing inward. Both share the goal: change the database or the UI without touching business rules. Cost: more files, more interfaces. Worth it for long-lived complex systems.

chunks.title: Event-driven architecture
chunks.text: Services communicate by publishing events to a message broker (Kafka, RabbitMQ, SNS) rather than calling each other directly. Producers don't know consumers. Benefits: decoupling, async processing, audit trail, easy fanout. Tradeoffs: eventual consistency, harder debugging (no stack trace across services), schema evolution requires care. Patterns: event sourcing, CQRS, saga (long-running transactions across services).

chunks.title: CQRS and event sourcing
chunks.text: CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) separates the write model (commands) from the read model (queries) — they can have different schemas optimized for each direction. Event sourcing stores changes as immutable events; current state is derived by replaying events. Benefits: audit trail, time travel, multiple read projections. Costs: complexity, eventual consistency between write and read sides. Often combined with CQRS.

chunks.title: Distributed system patterns
chunks.text: Common patterns for distributed systems: circuit breaker (stop hammering a failing service), bulkhead (isolate resources so one failure can't take everything down), retry with exponential backoff and jitter, idempotency keys (safe retries), saga (compensating actions for failed steps in a long workflow), leader election (one node coordinates), sidecar (helper container next to your service for cross-cutting concerns).

chunks.title: Performance testing
chunks.text: Load testing measures throughput and latency under expected traffic; stress testing pushes beyond capacity to find the breaking point; soak testing runs for hours to surface memory leaks. Tools: k6, JMeter, Locust, Gatling. Useful metrics: p50/p95/p99 latency (averages hide tail behaviour), requests per second, error rate. Always test against production-like data sizes — performance bugs that vanish at small scale often dominate at large scale.

chunks.title: Mutation testing
chunks.text: Mutation testing measures test quality by injecting small changes (mutations) into your code and checking if tests catch them. If a mutated version passes all tests, your tests have a gap. Tools: PIT (Java), Stryker (JS), mutmut (Python). It's slower and more expensive than line coverage but a much better signal — high line coverage with low mutation score means tests are exercising code without checking behaviour.

chunks.title: Property-based testing
chunks.text: Property-based testing generates random inputs and checks that a property holds for all of them (e.g. 'reversing a list twice gives the original'). Hypothesis (Python), QuickCheck (Haskell), fast-check (JS). When a property fails, the framework shrinks the failing input to the smallest example. Catches edge cases hand-written tests miss — empty inputs, integer overflow, Unicode quirks. Complements example-based tests.

chunks.title: A/B testing infrastructure
chunks.text: A/B testing assigns users randomly to a control and one or more variants, measures a metric, and tests for statistical significance. Building it: deterministic assignment by user hash, exposure logging, an experiment registry, statistical analysis. Pitfalls: sample size too small (false negative), peeking at results early (false positive), multiple comparisons inflating type-I error, novelty effects. Mature platforms (Optimizely, in-house at scale) handle these.

chunks.title: Monitoring and observability
chunks.text: Monitoring is checking known unknowns (CPU > 90%? alert). Observability is debugging unknown unknowns from system output. The three pillars: metrics (time-series numbers), logs (text events), traces (request flow across services). Tools: Prometheus + Grafana for metrics, Loki/ELK for logs, Jaeger/Tempo for traces. Modern stacks unify them under OpenTelemetry. Cardinality (number of unique label combinations) is the silent killer of metrics systems.

chunks.title: Incident response and post-mortems
chunks.text: When production breaks, follow a runbook: identify the impact, declare an incident, communicate, mitigate (rollback or band-aid), then fix the root cause. After resolution, write a blameless post-mortem documenting timeline, root cause, contributing factors, action items. The goal is to prevent recurrence, not to assign blame. Google's SRE book is the canonical reference.

chunks.title: Technical debt
chunks.text: Technical debt is the cost of choosing a quick solution now over a better one that takes longer. Interest accumulates as future changes become harder. Healthy debt: deliberate shortcuts to ship faster, paid down when you have time. Bad debt: accidental complexity from sloppy code, unaware of what needs fixing. Tracking debt (issues tagged tech-debt) and dedicating capacity to it prevents the bankruptcy state where new features stall.

chunks.title: Software estimation
chunks.text: Estimating how long something takes is notoriously hard. Useful techniques: break large tasks into small ones (sum of small estimates beats one big one), use relative sizing (story points) for backlog grooming, track velocity over sprints to predict future, add buffer for unknowns, refuse to commit to deadlines for vague work. Hofstadter's law: it always takes longer than you expect, even when you take Hofstadter's law into account.
